sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 65-year-old male, presents with a complaint of shortness of breath for approximately one week.
He reports that this is a recurrence of symptoms he experienced six months ago, which improved with an increase in his Lasix dose.
He has a history of coronary disease, including a heart attack in 2018 and a stent placement in his right coronary artery.
His ejection fraction was reported to be 35%.
He has stage 3 chronic kidney disease and has been taking Lasix 40mg once daily.
He reports swelling in his legs and nocturia, but denies changes in urination frequency.
He has been taking Motrin for occasional pain, but has been advised to avoid it due to his kidney function.
He is also experiencing foot pain, which he attributes to a recent episode of pain in both feet, lasting 4-5 days.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of coronary disease, including a heart attack in 2018, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease.
He has been advised to avoid Motrin and Advil due to his kidney function.
Review of systems:
The patient reports shortness of breath, particularly with walking or stairs, and nocturia.
He also reports swelling in his legs.
Current medications:
Lasix 40mg once daily
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 135/80
Physical exam:
Jugular venous distention up to 9cm
Bibasilar crackles with occasional rhonchi on lung exam
3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base on heart exam
1-2+ pitting edema bilaterally on lower extremity exam
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an acute heart failure exacerbation, likely due to fluid retention, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease.
Problem list: Heart failure exacerbation; stage 3 chronic kidney disease; possible gout flare; coronary artery disease; possible kidney dysfunction.
Plan:
I increased the patient's Lasix dose to 80mg once daily, to be taken in the morning, with a possible second dose in the afternoon.
I also ordered a chest x-ray and an echocardiogram to assess the heart murmur and ejection fraction.
I referred the patient to a dietitian to discuss dietary changes to manage his salt intake.
I advised him to avoid Motrin and Advil and stick to Tylenol for pain management.
I also ordered a complete metabolic panel to assess his kidney function.
Follow-up:
The patient will call me in 2 days to report on his weight and any changes in symptoms.
He will also be seen by his nephrologist, Dr. White, for further management of his kidney disease.
